# Questions in Livestream 45

Live Stream #45: Amulets and Rumors of Amulets.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=320" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:20</a> | What is the rapture? | He argues for a rapture understood as summary judgment rather than a pre-tribulational rescue. | He says his view changed with his hermeneutics. He reads the "taken" language alongside judgment parables such as wheat and tares, fish in a net, Noah, and Revelation's winepress imagery, so the rapture is the removal or judgment of the condemned rather than a secret pre-tribulational rescue of believers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=644" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:44</a> | If humanity establishes space colonies, would those settlements count as part of the `adamah`? | He says the mandate to subdue creation is not obviously limited to Earth, though practical space settlement remains speculative. | He frames the mandate as applying to the created order wherever humans set foot, so an `adamah` could include whatever environment humanity inhabits. He adds that the Bible does not forbid subduing Mars or another world, but practical colonies remain unlikely for now because closed ecosystems and long-term off-world survival are extremely difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=840" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:00</a> | How would you show that biblical cosmology and "pleasing aroma" language are analogical rather than literal? | He appeals to obviously figurative texts, saying Scripture often uses analogy and imagery rather than flat literalism. | He points to texts where literalism plainly fails: God sheltering people under wings, faithfulness as a shield, windows of heaven, humans as images, and Christ's sword from his mouth in Revelation. In the same way, "pleasing aroma" does not require God to have a literal nose; Scripture uses familiar created images to speak about divine realities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=1260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:00</a> | Does documented history show Nebuchadnezzar fulfilled Ezekiel 26:7-11, with Alexander fitting later verses? | He says yes: Nebuchadnezzar fits the initial fulfillment, while the chapter also allows repeated conquests such as Alexander's. | He identifies Ezekiel 26:7-11 with Nebuchadnezzar II and says Babylonian evidence points to both preparation for Tyre's conquest and Tyre as a Babylonian province afterward. For Alexander, he appeals to the chapter's "many nations" wave imagery, reading Tyre's history as repeated assaults that culminate in Alexander's final conquest. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=1641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:21</a> | Do Ezekiel 26:14, 26:2, and 26:21 mean Tyre would never be rebuilt, or never rebuilt the same way? | He says the point is that Tyre would never return as the same regional power, not that no rebuilding would ever happen. | He reads the passages as saying Tyre's former power and status would be spent. He links the judgment to Tyre's failure to aid Judah under an older mutual-defense relationship and says the city could be partially rebuilt between conquests, but never again as the same regional power before its final loss. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=1847" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:47</a> | What do the 70 years concerning Tyre mean in Isaiah 23:15-18, and are they connected to Ezekiel 26? | He connects them to exile and later return under Babylonian policy, with overlap in theme but not as a simple one-text explanation. | He compares Isaiah's wording about the Lord visiting Tyre after 70 years with Jeremiah 29's 70-year Babylonian exile language. He explains it as exile and return under Babylonian population-transfer policy: Tyre is judged and exiled, then visited and allowed to return, but resumes its pagan commercial ways. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=2154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:54</a> | In Luke 19:44, was Jesus speaking hyperbolically about Jerusalem's destruction? | He says the saying points to the Roman destruction of Jerusalem, with some hyperbolic force even though the devastation was real. | He reads Jesus' words as a prediction of the Roman siege in AD 70, comparable in kind to Nebuchadnezzar's earlier destruction. "Not one stone" has some hyperbolic force because a few remains such as the Western Wall survived, but he says the Romans were thorough enough that the warning remains substantially true. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=2375" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:35</a> | Are Solomon's 700 wives and 300 concubines symbolic or literal? | He treats them as rounded figures expressing a very large royal harem tied to treaties, trade, and political power. | He treats the numbers as rounded rather than exact, but still conveying an enormous harem. He explains the wives as treaty wives tied to smaller rulers and trade routes, with concubines producing royal daughters for diplomatic exchange, making Solomon's wealth, treaties, horses, and later idolatry part of the same political-economic system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=2722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:22</a> | What are the top five most important sites related to biblical archaeology? | He gives a ranked-style answer centered on major sites such as Qumran and other key locations for biblical history and texts. | He names Qumran for the Dead Sea Scrolls, Jerusalem for Davidic and temple history, Avaris for Exodus background, Tel Dan for the House of David inscription and temple-like remains, and Hazor for conquest evidence. He also mentions Ashkelon, Hebron, and Gezer as important additional sites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=3132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:12</a> | Is Noah named Noah because he inherited the name from an earlier clan progenitor? | He says the evidence is uncertain, but the clan-progenitor model is possible and worth considering. | He says clan names can come from an original progenitor or from a later figure retrojected as the clan's defining ancestor. Noah may belong near the later part of a long clan span and receive the whole clan's credit because he saves humanity through the ark, while his sons mark the splitting of the clan into new lines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=3695" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:35</a> | Have you ever considered leading a tour of Egypt? | Yes; a prior tour was planned but canceled for lack of signups, and he would consider trying again if the channel grew enough. | He says a tour company had previously asked him to lead an Egypt tour, but the channel could not produce the roughly 12 or 13 signups needed, so it was canceled. He would like to try again with a larger audience and even imagines a long route from Aswan to Alexandria. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=3981" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:21</a> | Do you have or recommend any sources on the plagues of Egypt? | He says there is a scholarly gap; Kenneth Kitchen is the best stopgap, though he wants a more detailed book on the plagues. | He says there is no single good book on the plagues that he can recommend, which is why he wanted to write one. As a stopgap he recommends Kenneth Kitchen, while saying more detailed work is needed on Egyptian context, illnesses, naturalistic elements, miracles, and the plagues' broader meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=4201" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:01</a> | Who sold Joseph into slavery, his brothers or the Midianites/Ishmaelites? | Both: the brothers sold him first, then the traders resold him in Egypt. | He says Genesis 37 makes the brothers the first sellers and the Ishmaelite or Midianite traders the first buyers. Those traders then take Joseph to Egypt and sell him again to Potiphar, so the apparent tension is resolved by two sales in sequence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=4505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:05</a> | How does the Old Testament compare with other ancient historical accounts of a people group? | It spans a long period with shifting cultural contexts; Israelite practice overlaps with Egypt and Canaan but also differs in diet, ritual, and theology. | He stresses that the Old Testament spans about a thousand years, so comparison changes by period. In Egypt, Israelites look very Egyptian in practices such as red bread bowls, birthing bricks, and reed baskets; in Canaan they adopt some Canaanite practices, but diet, ritual, theology, and specific laws also mark real differences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=5098" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:58</a> | Could you explain Moses' genealogy: was Amram son of Kohath or from the clan of Kohath, and where does Jochebed fit? | He reads Exodus 6 as clan language, with Amram and Kohath as clan/progenitor markers and Jochebed as a related branch-family member. | He says Exodus 6 explicitly uses clan language, so Levi, Kohath, and Amram should be read as clan or progenitor markers rather than a simple three-generation chain. Jochebed is probably from a related branch family within the clan, making the relationship distant rather than incestuous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=5494" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:34</a> | Does Horemheb fit the profile of the pharaoh who wanted to kill Moses? | Yes; he sees Horemheb's anti-corruption, law-and-order profile as fitting a pharaoh who would pursue Moses for killing the taskmaster. | He describes Horemheb as an outsider to the corrupt Amarna system who became deeply committed to punishing wrongdoing, even among elites. That anti-corruption and law-and-order profile makes him, in Falk's view, a fitting candidate for the king who would pursue Moses over the killing of the taskmaster. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=5819" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:59</a> | Was Tanis destroyed by fire, as some videos claim from burned-looking statues? | He is unsure; he saw a destruction layer, but not burned statues, and says the evidence is limited and unpublished. | He says he saw a clear destruction layer at Tanis, probably in a granary area, but he does not know whether it extended across the city or temple district. He did not see burned statues, only desert varnish, and notes that the relevant evidence is limited, active, and not fully published. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=6012" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:12</a> | Were Old Testament laws enforced more or less strictly than laws today? | It varied by law: some were cultural norms, some strictly enforced, and some poorly enforced. | He says enforcement depends on the law and what counts as enforcement. High-place worship, teraphim, dressed-stone altars, child sacrifice, and some food laws were inconsistently enforced, while mediums and incest laws appear stricter; other practices, like avoiding pork, worked more as cultural norms than as constant legal policing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=6415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:55</a> | Was the golden calf meant as an idol to Yahweh, and should Exodus 32:4 read "these are your gods"? | He says the Hebrew points to plural gods; Aaron places a bovine deity beside Yahweh, violating the covenant. | After checking the Hebrew, he says the demonstrative and verb support "these are your gods." He reads Aaron as adding a bovine deity alongside Yahweh rather than identifying the calf with Yahweh, which violates the covenant command not to have other gods in Yahweh's presence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=6986" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:26</a> | Why is evidence for David and Solomon difficult to find from the 11th and 10th centuries BC? | Wet conditions destroy perishable evidence; enough remains support historical David and Solomon, though kingdom size remains debated. | He says the main problem is moisture: rain and wet soil destroy papyri, parchment, and other perishable evidence, as in the Nile Delta and Jerusalem. He says enough Iron Age evidence now supports historical David and Solomon, with the real debate centered on the size and reach of their kingdoms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=7298" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:38</a> | Is it feasible for Solomon to have had so many wives, and are there ancient parallels? | Yes; large royal harems tied to treaty networks have parallels, especially in Egyptian royal practice, though exact numbers are uncertain. | He says it is feasible if the kingdom had enough resources and a large enough diplomatic network. He compares treaty wives and large harems to Egyptian royal practice under rulers such as Ramesses II and Amenhotep III, while emphasizing that such harems were costly to house, feed, clothe, and staff. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=7562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:02</a> | Do you recommend English translations of Egyptian texts for general readers? | He recommends William Kelly Simpson's *Ancient Egyptian Literature* and is more cautious about Miriam Lichtheim. | He recommends William Kelly Simpson's *Ancient Egyptian Literature* as a good one-volume translation for general readers. He says Miriam Lichtheim is important and not the worst option, but he finds some of her translations too free or artistically shaped compared with Simpson. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=7728" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:48</a> | Was King Abijam/Abijah's maternal grandfather Absalom? | Yes, he says the names line up and Abijah is related to David through both paternal and maternal lines. | He checks the names in 1 Kings 15 and 2 Samuel 3 and says Abishalom and Absalom line up. On that reading, Abijam or Abijah is related to David through Rehoboam on the paternal side and through Absalom on the maternal side. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=8144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:44</a> | Is there any evidence for Moses? | No direct inscriptional evidence is expected, but he points to indirect evidence from Moses' Egyptian name, social role, protest parallels, and Exodus itself. | He says direct Egyptian inscriptions for Moses should not be expected because Moses would have been a marginal or pariah figure. He points instead to indirect evidence: the Egyptian character of the name, parallels with Late Bronze Age protest intermediaries, Moses' standing before both Israel and Pharaoh, and Exodus itself as an ancient source. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=8515" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:55</a> | Are there good Exodus evidence series or documentaries? | He points to his own series and Inspiring Philosophy, but says most commercial Exodus documentaries are archaeologically poor. | He prefers "evidence" to "proof" and points to his own unfinished Exodus series and Inspiring Philosophy's documentary work, which he helped with. He says many commercial Exodus documentaries are visually strong but archaeologically weak, including popular treatments of Sinai or older speculative proposals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=8830" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:10</a> | Have you ever tried buza, the modern Egyptian version of ancient Egyptian beer? | No; he has tried Egyptian beer generally, but not buza specifically. | He says he has tried Egyptian beer while in Egypt, but not buza specifically. He explains that Egyptian beer is available to tourists and Copts, says he did not especially like the watery domestic beer, but adds that any cold beer can be welcome after a day in extreme desert heat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=8966" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:26</a> | Was bilharzia endemic to ancient Egypt and interpreted as male menstruation? | He cannot answer the epidemiology directly, but discusses Nile parasites, unsafe water, and beer as safer hydration. | He says he cannot answer the specific epidemiology or "male menstruation" claim. Instead, he emphasizes that the Nile carried many parasites and diseases, that drinking or swimming in Nile water was dangerous, and that Egyptians learned wells and especially beer were safer sources of hydration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=9300" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:00</a> | What is the evidence for and against Israelites originating from Canaanite heritage versus an Exodus out of Egypt? | He argues the Egyptian vocabulary, customs, route details, Avaris, Hazor, and Merneptah Stele favor an Egyptian origin over peaceful Canaanite emergence. | He argues that a purely Canaanite origin struggles to explain Egyptian vocabulary, customs, route details, knowledge of Pi-Ramesses, Avaris emptying, and the 19th-dynasty fit of the narratives. He also cites Hazor's destruction and the Merneptah Stele's early reference to Israel as evidence that weighs against a slow late Canaanite emergence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=9755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:35</a> | How long was Horemheb's reign: 14, 19, or 28 years? | He says current majority thinking favors 14 years, based on wine docket evidence, though some scholars argue longer. | He says the latest majority view is about 14 years, mainly because wine dockets from Horemheb's tomb appear to reach only year 13, implying he died before another harvest. He notes that Kenneth Kitchen and Aidan Dodson have argued for longer reigns, but expects the shorter view to continue gaining ground. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=10078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:58</a> | If early biblical genealogies are clans rather than individual ages, how should Genesis 6:3 be read? | He reads the 120 years as the countdown to the flood and the limit of God's patience, not a maximum human lifespan. | He takes Genesis 6:3 as a countdown before judgment rather than a new maximum human lifespan. The point is that God will not strive with violent humanity forever; the 120 years mark the limit of divine patience before the flood wipes out the clans and starts over. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=10507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:07</a> | What do you think about *Moon Knight* and its use of Egyptian mythology and history? | He has not seen it yet, but says popular media can generate interest in Egyptology if it is well done. | He says he had only recently heard of *Moon Knight* and had not watched it, so he cannot evaluate its mythology or history. More generally, he says popular media can spark interest in Egyptology, comparing it to how even a campy *Doctor Who* episode helped stir his own interest as a teenager. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=10640" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:20</a> | Have you considered using Discord for community interaction outside livestreams? | Yes, but moderation time and resources are the obstacle; he may reconsider if help is available. | He says they have considered a Discord server for documents, resources, pictures, and community interaction, but time and moderation are the main barriers. Their priority remains educational content because he and his producer lack the resources to moderate another platform. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=10827" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:27</a> | Are there any good video sources against the documentary hypothesis? | He says books are stronger than videos here, and points listeners toward authors such as Joshua Berman and earlier critics. | He says he does not know much in video form and recommends books and articles instead. He names Joshua Berman's *Inconsistency in the Torah*, Kenneth Kitchen, Cassuto, and other critics, while adding that opposition to the documentary hypothesis has existed for a long time and is not limited to theological conservatives. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=11102" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:02</a> | Is 120 years symbolic, and why did God wait longer to judge Canaan? | The 120 years may be symbolic as a multiple of 40, but the main point is God's limited patience; judgment varies by sin, context, covenant responsibility, and divine patience. | He says 120 could be symbolic because it is a multiple of 40, as with Moses' age language, but its function is still a line in the sand. Canaan's judgment differs because not all sins, contexts, or covenant responsibilities are the same, and God's patience ends at different points depending on the situation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=11850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:30</a> | What does archaeology tell us about Sodom and Gomorrah? Did they exist? | He thinks they did exist, but says they have not been securely identified archaeologically. | He thinks Sodom and Gomorrah existed, probably on the southeast side of the Dead Sea near known tar pits, but says no secure identification has been made. He rejects Tall el-Hammam as Sodom, expects a Middle Bronze Age setting around the Abrahamic period, and notes that even major cities can remain undiscovered. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/K-auTwN2ygQ?t=12135" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:15</a> | Did Ramesses II have pets, and what kinds of pets did ancient Egyptians keep? | Yes. He mentions royal horses and dogs, common household cats, and a broader Egyptian fondness for trained animals. | He says Ramesses II had beloved chariot horses and that Egyptian elites also kept dogs, especially greyhounds. Commoners more often kept working cats for hunting and rodent control, while kings might also keep exotic animals such as baboons, cheetahs, leopards, birds, and small menageries. |
